claims.claim_text: The opening healthcare analytics company story is presented as a real platform-team case study without attribution.
claims.anchor_text: A platform team at a healthcare analytics company inherited a problem that looked like a machine learning bottleneck but was really a storage architecture failure.
claims.suggested_rewrite: A platform team supporting data-intensive workloads can inherit a problem that looks like a machine learning bottleneck but is really a storage architecture failure.
claims.rationale: The scenario is useful pedagogically, but no primary source identifies this real company/story, so the real-world framing should be removed.

claims.claim_text: MinIO is high-performance S3-compatible object storage.
claims.span_hint: section: MinIO's Job in a Kubernetes Platform
claims.rationale: The MinIO upstream README directly describes MinIO as an S3-compatible object storage solution.

claims.claim_text: S3 workflows include operations such as PutObject, GetObject, multipart upload, bucket lifecycle, and bucket/object policy operations.
claims.span_hint: section: MinIO's Job in a Kubernetes Platform
claims.rationale: The Amazon S3 API reference directly lists the named S3 actions, including GetObject, PutObject, multipart-upload actions, lifecycle configuration, and policy actions.

claims.claim_text: MinIO erasure coding uses data/parity shards and checksums to recover from drive failures and silent corruption when enough shards remain.
claims.span_hint: section: Core Architecture: API, Erasure Coding, and Server Pools
claims.rationale: The MinIO erasure-code guide directly states that MinIO uses erasure code and checksums to protect against hardware failures and corruption.

claims.claim_text: By default, MinIO shards objects across N/2 data and N/2 parity drives; an eight-drive example can be represented as four data shards and four parity shards.
claims.rationale: The MinIO erasure-code guide directly documents the N/2 data and N/2 parity default, which supports the eight-drive 4+4 example.

claims.claim_text: Kubernetes topology spread constraints can distribute Pods across failure domains such as nodes, zones, and regions to improve availability.
claims.rationale: The Kubernetes documentation directly says topology spread constraints control Pod distribution across failure domains such as regions, zones, and nodes.

claims.claim_text: A MinIO Operator Tenant represents an independent MinIO object store and the Operator generates PVCs and services for the tenant.
claims.span_hint: section: Deploying MinIO with the Operator
claims.rationale: The MinIO Operator README directly describes Tenants as independent MinIO object stores and documents generated PVCs and tenant services.

claims.claim_text: MinIO Tenants deploy with TLS enabled by default using Kubernetes certificates.k8s.io certificates signed by the Kubernetes CA, and Pods do not trust that CA by default.
claims.span_hint: section: Deploying MinIO with the Operator / TLS troubleshooting
claims.rationale: The MinIO Operator README directly states the default TLS behavior, Kubernetes certificates API usage, CA signing, and Pod trust limitation.

claims.claim_text: In virtual-hosted-style S3 addressing the bucket name is part of the hostname, while path-style addressing keeps the bucket in the URL path; Kubernetes Services have DNS names like service.namespace.svc.cluster-domain.example.
claims.span_hint: section: Worked Example: Debug a Broken MLflow Artifact Store
claims.rationale: The AWS S3 virtual hosting page directly defines virtual-hosted and path-style URL formats; Kubernetes DNS naming is separately documented at kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/services-networking/dns-pod-service/.

claims.claim_text: MLflow artifact stores support S3 and S3-compatible storage such as MinIO, and MLflow can use MLFLOW_S3_ENDPOINT_URL for a custom S3 endpoint.
claims.span_hint: section: Worked Example and Integrating MinIO with Platform Workloads
claims.rationale: The official MLflow artifact-store documentation directly supports the claim, but mlflow.org is not currently allowlisted.

claims.claim_text: Grafana Loki supports S3 object storage configuration with endpoint, bucketnames, access keys, insecure mode, and s3forcepathstyle/path-style addressing.
claims.span_hint: section: Integrating MinIO with Platform Workloads / Loki
claims.rationale: The Loki configuration reference directly documents the s3_storage_config fields including endpoint, bucketnames, credentials, insecure, and s3forcepathstyle.

claims.claim_text: Velero's AWS object-store plugin stores backups in S3, supports s3ForcePathStyle and s3Url for local services such as MinIO, and plugin v1.10.x is compatible with Velero v1.14.x.
claims.span_hint: section: Integrating MinIO with Platform Workloads / Velero
claims.rationale: The Velero AWS plugin documentation directly describes backup object storage, s3ForcePathStyle, s3Url for MinIO/local storage services, and the README compatibility table documents v1.10.x to Velero v1.14.x.

claims.claim_text: Docker BuildKit's S3 cache backend supports use_path_style, which uses the bucket in the URL instead of the hostname.
claims.span_hint: section: Integrating MinIO with Platform Workloads / BuildKit
claims.rationale: The Docker Build cache S3 backend documentation directly defines the use_path_style option this way.

claims.claim_text: The MinIO metrics table names minio_s3_ttfb_seconds as the time-to-first-byte metric.
claims.span_hint: section: Monitoring, Capacity, and Recovery / KEY MINIO METRICS
claims.rationale: The current upstream MinIO metrics list documents minio_s3_requests_ttfb_seconds_distribution rather than minio_s3_ttfb_seconds.

claims.claim_text: MinIO exposes Prometheus metrics at /minio/v2/metrics/cluster and documents cluster capacity, drive, node, healing, S3 request, traffic, and bucket usage metrics.
claims.rationale: The upstream MinIO metrics list directly documents the cluster endpoint and the named metric families.

claims.claim_text: Ceph can provide block, file, and object interfaces through RBD, CephFS, and RGW.
claims.span_hint: section: When to Choose MinIO, Cloud S3, or Ceph
claims.rationale: The Ceph documentation mirrored on readthedocs.io directly states that the same cluster can operate RGW, CephFS, and Ceph block devices.

further_reading.title: MinIO upstream README
further_reading.why_relevant: Defines MinIO as S3-compatible object storage and links to related upstream documentation.

further_reading.title: MinIO Operator documentation
further_reading.why_relevant: Covers Tenant architecture, generated Kubernetes resources, PVC behavior, service access, and default TLS behavior.

further_reading.title: MinIO Erasure Code Quickstart
further_reading.why_relevant: Explains MinIO erasure coding, data/parity shards, checksums, and failure tolerance.

further_reading.title: Amazon S3 path-style and virtual-hosted-style URLs
further_reading.why_relevant: Provides the authoritative distinction behind the Kubernetes DNS/path-style debugging example.

further_reading.title: Grafana Loki configuration reference
further_reading.why_relevant: Documents Loki S3 storage fields including endpoint, bucketnames, insecure mode, and s3forcepathstyle.

notes: No shared section citation pool was found for this storage module path. I treated the opening healthcare story as an unattributed illustrative scenario rather than a citable incident, and flagged one MinIO metric name that appears outdated against current upstream metrics documentation.
